title: Safety and efficacy of CAR T-cell therapy in central nervous system lymphoma: a systematic review and meta-analysis
design: Systematic review and meta-analysis (PRISMA 2020; PROSPERO CRD420251070033) of 39 studies (38 in quantitative analysis; up to 1,190 patients). Predominantly retrospective single-arm cohorts (n=28) plus 11 single-arm interventional/phase 1-2 studies; NO randomized comparisons. Freeman-Tukey double-arcsine pooling, random-effects (DerSimonian-Laird).
population: Adults (>=18) with PCNSL or SCNSL treated with CD19-based CAR T-cell therapy (axi-cel, tisa-cel, liso-cel, brexu-cel, and dual/triple-targeted constructs). 20 studies SCNSL, 5 PCNSL, 13 both. Predominant histology DLBCL; includes double-/triple-hit subsets in several cohorts. No restriction on prior lines or bridging.
key_eligibility: Included adults with PCNSL or SCNSL (systemic NHL with CNS involvement) receiving CD19-based CAR-T; single- and dual/triple-targeted constructs allowed
Case reports with <3 patients, reviews, editorials, and preclinical studies EXCLUDED; required separately reported CNS-lymphoma outcomes
Both prospective and retrospective designs, including case series with >=3 patients; no restriction on prior therapy lines, bridging, or geography
Rationale: pivotal CAR-T trials systematically excluded active CNS involvement due to neurotoxicity concerns
outcomes: Pooled ORR 0.75 (95% CI 0.70-0.79; I2=53.3%); CR 0.52 (0.46-0.58); PR 0.18 (0.14-0.22). No significant difference in ORR between PCNSL and SCNSL (p=0.063). PFS pooled HR PCNSL vs SCNSL 0.89 (95% CI 0.16-4.91, NS). OS rate 0.71 at 6 mo to 0.48 at 24 mo; PFS 0.41 at 6 mo to 0.28 at 24 mo. Pooled median-of-medians OS 8.6 mo, PFS 3.6 mo. CRS 83.5% (grade>=3 5.77%); ICANS 44.9% (grade>=3 17.4%).
grounds_rubric_claims: Meta-analytic (non-randomized) evidence supports CAR T-cell therapy achieving robust responses in CNS lymphoma (ORR ~75%, CR ~52%)
Efficacy of CAR-T is comparable between primary and secondary CNS lymphoma
Severe CRS is infrequent (grade>=3 ~6%) and severe ICANS is manageable (~17%) in CNS-lymphoma CAR-T
Evidence base for CAR-T in CNS lymphoma is non-randomized (retrospective cohorts + single-arm trials), as CNS involvement was excluded from pivotal trials

verbatim_quotes.quote: The pooled overall response rate (ORR) was 0.75 [95% CI: 0.70-0.79]... Complete response (CR) rate was 0.52 [0.46-0.58]
verbatim_quotes.supports: Pooled CAR-T efficacy in CNS lymphoma: ORR 75%, CR 52%

verbatim_quotes.quote: CART therapy achieves robust response rates in CNS lymphoma, with efficacy comparable between PCNSL and SCNSL. Neurotoxicity remains frequent but manageable, and severe CRS events are infrequent.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Overall conclusion: comparable PCNSL/SCNSL efficacy; manageable neurotoxicity; infrequent severe CRS

verbatim_quotes.quote: Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) occurred in 83.5% [79.0-88.0], with grade>=3 CRS in 5.77% [3.0-9.0]. Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome (ICANS) was reported in 44.9% [36.0-54.0], with severe (grade >=3) events in 17.4% [12.0-23.0].

caveats: Non-randomized evidence: 28/39 studies retrospective single-arm; substantial heterogeneity (ICANS I2=84%). PFS HR comparison PCNSL vs SCNSL very wide (0.16-4.91). Pooled proportions across heterogeneous products, prior-line burden, and bridging. Registry/abstract data included.
